# Ex09 Analysis of Lecture Recording

## Conclusion

Our data supports the proposal to livestream the in-person COMP 110 lectures. There is broad demand among all demographics, with a emphasized demand in subgroups that have the biggest barriers for in-person attendance. None of the subgroups we analyzed had any opposition to the change (mean<3.5). There are risks associated with a livestream adoption, including an even smaller proportion of attendance (already less than 75% average) and passive learning without engaging in in-class activities like memory-diagram writing, but those tradeoffs are smaller than the increase in engagement and opportunity to catch up in learning. This change can specifically help students that may struggle to attend or struggle with course content the most. For instance, we found that 75% of seniors, a group that has the highest likelihood of commuting to UNC, reported a rating of 5 or higher, and 75+% of students that listed no in response to being a computer science major also reported a rating of 5 or higher out of 7. This suggests these groups believe that having recordings of lecture would help them with succeeding in the course. There are also some costs associated with the addition, including instructor adaptation, cost of transcription and recording device installation, etc.. To begin with implementation, we could design a pilot specifically for students that had a documented reason to be excused, such as medical or family leave, and survey if they thought it was a good addition to the course. Our data finally suggests that students have a desire for livestreaming, but in future studies we need to collect data on whether the recording supplemental actually harms or improves scores, and if it increases or decreases disparities in grade outcomes.
